after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation
before_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in before['nodes']}
after_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in after['nodes']}

# Check all nodes for changes
for i in sorted set of ids
all_ids = set(list(before_nodes.keys()) + list(after_nodes.keys()))
changed = []
for i in all_ids:
    bc = before_nodes.get(i, ac = after_nodes.get(i)
    if bc and ac and bc['color'] != ac['color']:
        changed.append((i, bc['color'], ac['color']))
    elif bc and not ac:
        changed.append((i, bc['color'], '], 'gone'))
    elif not bc and ac:
        changed.append((i, 'new', ac['color']))
print("Changed:")
for c for c in changed:
    print(c)
</parameter>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting - clicking on the green square (id=22) caused the bottom row to shift! The colors shifted:
- id=23: N->W (green became white - selected?)
- id=24: p->N (purple became green)
- id=25: b->p (blue became purple)
- id=26: Y->b (yellow became blue)
- id=27: new Y (new yellow appeared)
So clicking on a bottom square also shifts the row, but the clicked square becomes white (selected). The row shifts left with the clicked position becoming white, and a new color appears at the end.
So the mechanic seems to be:
1. SPACE shifts the bottom row left
2. Clicking on a bottom square selects it (white border) and shifts the row.
Now I need to figure out the goal. The top row has 4 colored boxes: blue, green, yellow, purple.green, yellow, purple. The middle has 4 gray squares. The bottom has 4 colored squares.
Maybe the goal is to fill the gray squares with the correct colors matching the top order. Let me try clicking on one of the gray squares in the middle to fill them with the selected color.
